channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: Buyer demand remains weak as housing market struggles for momentum
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Housing Market Shows Tentative Signs of Stabilisation

The UK housing market remains subdued, though recent data suggests the pace of decline may be easing. According to the latest RICS Residential Market Survey, buyer demand and agreed sales continue to weaken, reflecting ongoing economic uncertainty and higher mortgage costs. However, May marked the first month since January where conditions did not deteriorate further, potentially indicating the market is finding its floor.

New buyer enquiries held steady at a net balance of -34%, matching April's figure, whilst agreed sales remained negative at -37%. Though these metrics still point to challenging conditions, the lack of additional decline offers a glimmer of stability. Transaction times have lengthened considerably, with properties now taking an average of 21.5 weeks from listing to completion – the longest delay since records began in 2017.

Regional Variations and Rental Market Pressures

House prices continue to face downward pressure, with a net balance of -35% of surveyors reporting declines. The South East and East Anglia emerged as the weakest regions, whilst Northern Ireland bucked the trend with continued price growth. Looking ahead, sentiment appears cautiously optimistic, with the 12-month sales outlook turning slightly positive at +2%.

The rental market tells a different story, with demand outstripping supply. Tenant demand remained robust at +14%, whilst landlord instructions dwindled further to -28%. This imbalance has pushed rent expectations to +36%, their highest level in twelve months, suggesting further increases loom for tenants.

Experts Urge Caution Amid Economic Uncertainty

Tarrant Parsons, RICS Head of Market Research, noted: "Whilst the latest figures hint at stabilisation, key indicators remain in negative territory. It would be premature to declare this the start of a recovery." The recent dip in inflation provided temporary relief, but with energy costs still feeding through to prices, the Bank of England may yet implement further rate rises.

For those considering entering the market, these mixed signals highlight the importance of careful financial planning. Mortgage borrowers might wish to explore fixed-rate options to hedge against potential future increases, whilst landlords may need to weigh rising rental yields against tighter regulations and higher borrowing costs.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Housing Market Shows Tentative Signs of Stabilisation

The UK housing market remains subdued, though recent data suggests the pace of decline may be easing. According to the latest RICS Residential Market Survey, buyer demand and agreed sales continue to weaken, reflecting ongoing economic uncertainty and higher mortgage costs. However, May marked the first month since January where conditions did not deteriorate further, potentially indicating the market is finding its floor.

New buyer enquiries held steady at a net balance of -34%, matching April's figure, whilst agreed sales remained negative at -37%. Though these metrics still point to challenging conditions, the lack of additional decline offers a glimmer of stability. Transaction times have lengthened considerably, with properties now taking an average of 21.5 weeks from listing to completion – the longest delay since records began in 2017.

Regional Variations and Rental Market Pressures

House prices continue to face downward pressure, with a net balance of -35% of surveyors reporting declines. The South East and East Anglia emerged as the weakest regions, whilst Northern Ireland bucked the trend with continued price growth. Looking ahead, sentiment appears cautiously optimistic, with the 12-month sales outlook turning slightly positive at +2%.

The rental market tells a different story, with demand outstripping supply. Tenant demand remained robust at +14%, whilst landlord instructions dwindled further to -28%. This imbalance has pushed rent expectations to +36%, their highest level in twelve months, suggesting further increases loom for tenants.

Experts Urge Caution Amid Economic Uncertainty

Tarrant Parsons, RICS Head of Market Research, noted: "Whilst the latest figures hint at stabilisation, key indicators remain in negative territory. It would be premature to declare this the start of a recovery." The recent dip in inflation provided temporary relief, but with energy costs still feeding through to prices, the Bank of England may yet implement further rate rises.

For those considering entering the market, these mixed signals highlight the importance of careful financial planning. Mortgage borrowers might wish to explore fixed-rate options to hedge against potential future increases, whilst landlords may need to weigh rising rental yields against tighter regulations and higher borrowing costs.

item.title: Navigating compliance charges under the Renters’ Rights reforms
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Renters’ Rights Act has introduced significant changes to the private rental sector, with many landlords now facing unexpected compliance costs. Reports suggest letting agents are charging between £200 and £400 for updating tenancy paperwork to meet new legal requirements, sparking debate about fairness and transparency in these additional fees.

Understanding the compliance costs

Landlords across the UK have voiced concerns about rising expenses linked to the Renters’ Rights reforms. Whilst some view these charges as excessive, industry experts note that legitimate costs may arise from:

  • Updating tenancy agreements and documentation
  • Implementing new procedures for tenant communications
  • Training staff on revised legal requirements
  • Distributing mandatory government information sheets

For larger agencies managing hundreds of properties, these changes require considerable administrative work. Letting agents also face their own regulatory obligations, with potential penalties for non-compliance adding pressure to ensure all processes meet the new standards.

Assessing fee fairness and transparency

The key consideration for landlords isn’t whether fees can be charged, but whether they’re reasonable and properly justified. Property owners might wish to ask their agents to clarify:

  • Which specific services the fees cover
  • How the charges relate to their existing management agreement
  • The basis for the fee calculation
  • Whether the cost reflects the actual work involved

Many management contracts predate the Renters’ Rights Act, meaning they may not explicitly address these new compliance requirements. This grey area has led to some confusion about what constitutes fair additional charges versus unnecessary admin fees.

As the rental sector continues to adapt to these reforms, open communication between landlords and agents remains crucial to navigating compliance costs effectively. Both parties may benefit from reviewing agreements to ensure expectations around legislative changes are clearly outlined.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Renters’ Rights Act has introduced significant changes to the private rental sector, with many landlords now facing unexpected compliance costs. Reports suggest letting agents are charging between £200 and £400 for updating tenancy paperwork to meet new legal requirements, sparking debate about fairness and transparency in these additional fees.

Understanding the compliance costs

Landlords across the UK have voiced concerns about rising expenses linked to the Renters’ Rights reforms. Whilst some view these charges as excessive, industry experts note that legitimate costs may arise from:

  • Updating tenancy agreements and documentation
  • Implementing new procedures for tenant communications
  • Training staff on revised legal requirements
  • Distributing mandatory government information sheets

For larger agencies managing hundreds of properties, these changes require considerable administrative work. Letting agents also face their own regulatory obligations, with potential penalties for non-compliance adding pressure to ensure all processes meet the new standards.

Assessing fee fairness and transparency

The key consideration for landlords isn’t whether fees can be charged, but whether they’re reasonable and properly justified. Property owners might wish to ask their agents to clarify:

  • Which specific services the fees cover
  • How the charges relate to their existing management agreement
  • The basis for the fee calculation
  • Whether the cost reflects the actual work involved

Many management contracts predate the Renters’ Rights Act, meaning they may not explicitly address these new compliance requirements. This grey area has led to some confusion about what constitutes fair additional charges versus unnecessary admin fees.

As the rental sector continues to adapt to these reforms, open communication between landlords and agents remains crucial to navigating compliance costs effectively. Both parties may benefit from reviewing agreements to ensure expectations around legislative changes are clearly outlined.

item.title: Rental stock shortages continue to drive rent growth
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Rental prices in the UK’s most affordable areas are surging at more than double the national rate, according to fresh market analysis. Despite tenant demand cooling to a six-year low, a chronic shortage of available properties continues to push rents upwards, particularly in lower-cost regions.

Affordable areas see sharpest rent rises

Recent data reveals rents are climbing by an average of 5% in areas where monthly payments fall below £750 – significantly outpacing the 2.1% national growth rate. Locations including Carlisle (9.1%), Kilmarnock (9.0%) and Halifax (6.5%) have experienced particularly steep increases, though their rents remain below UK averages.

The rental stock shortage appears widespread, with every UK region still showing 20-30% fewer available properties compared to pre-pandemic levels. Industry experts suggest this stems from reduced investment in the private rental sector, restricting tenant options and maintaining upward pricing pressure.

Mixed picture across UK regions

While affordability has improved nationally – with wages growing 4% against 2.1% rental inflation for 18 consecutive months – regional variations remain stark. Several major cities including Bournemouth, Nottingham and Birmingham have actually seen rents decline over the past year, as affordability limits and softer demand constrained landlords’ pricing power.

London presents a notable exception, being the sole region where tenant enquiries increased (up 6% annually). The capital’s average rents now stand at £2,206 per month, with growth accelerating to 2.2% – slightly above the national rate.

Market indicators suggest some relief for renters, with the average enquiries per listing dropping to 5.6 – the lowest since 2020 and well below 2022’s peak of 16. However, analysts caution that without significant new rental supply entering the market, current affordability improvements may prove temporary. Forecasts suggest rents could rise a further 2-3% before year-end.

For those considering their housing options, the regional variations highlight the importance of researching local market conditions. Whilst some areas show signs of easing pressure, others continue to experience significant rental growth, particularly in traditionally more affordable locations.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Rental prices in the UK’s most affordable areas are surging at more than double the national rate, according to fresh market analysis. Despite tenant demand cooling to a six-year low, a chronic shortage of available properties continues to push rents upwards, particularly in lower-cost regions.

Affordable areas see sharpest rent rises

Recent data reveals rents are climbing by an average of 5% in areas where monthly payments fall below £750 – significantly outpacing the 2.1% national growth rate. Locations including Carlisle (9.1%), Kilmarnock (9.0%) and Halifax (6.5%) have experienced particularly steep increases, though their rents remain below UK averages.

The rental stock shortage appears widespread, with every UK region still showing 20-30% fewer available properties compared to pre-pandemic levels. Industry experts suggest this stems from reduced investment in the private rental sector, restricting tenant options and maintaining upward pricing pressure.

Mixed picture across UK regions

While affordability has improved nationally – with wages growing 4% against 2.1% rental inflation for 18 consecutive months – regional variations remain stark. Several major cities including Bournemouth, Nottingham and Birmingham have actually seen rents decline over the past year, as affordability limits and softer demand constrained landlords’ pricing power.

London presents a notable exception, being the sole region where tenant enquiries increased (up 6% annually). The capital’s average rents now stand at £2,206 per month, with growth accelerating to 2.2% – slightly above the national rate.

Market indicators suggest some relief for renters, with the average enquiries per listing dropping to 5.6 – the lowest since 2020 and well below 2022’s peak of 16. However, analysts caution that without significant new rental supply entering the market, current affordability improvements may prove temporary. Forecasts suggest rents could rise a further 2-3% before year-end.

For those considering their housing options, the regional variations highlight the importance of researching local market conditions. Whilst some areas show signs of easing pressure, others continue to experience significant rental growth, particularly in traditionally more affordable locations.

item.title: Unlicensed landlord ordered to pay more than £10,000
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

A landlord in Chester has been fined over £10,000 after being found guilty of managing rental properties without the necessary licence. Carl Southall, 60, was convicted at Mold Magistrates’ Court for 15 offences related to unlicensed property management activities across multiple locations. This case serves as a reminder of the importance of adhering to legal requirements in the rental market.

Details of the Offences

The offences took place in Shotton, Connah’s Quay, and Queensferry, dating back to March 2025. Southall was found to have collected rent from tenants at properties on Wellington Street in Shotton, High Street in Connah’s Quay, and Chester Road West in Queensferry, despite not holding the required licence. Initially, Southall pleaded guilty but later withdrew his pleas, leading the case to proceed to trial. Magistrates imposed fines of £450 for each of the 15 offences, amounting to a total of £6,750.

Financial Penalties and Repayment

In addition to the fines, Southall was ordered to pay £1,750 in prosecution costs and a victim surcharge of £2,000. This brings the overall amount payable to £10,500. The court has mandated that this sum be repaid at a rate of £1,750 per month. This case highlights the financial consequences that can arise from failing to comply with property management regulations.

Landlords and property managers may wish to ensure they hold the appropriate licences and adhere to all legal requirements to avoid similar penalties. This case underscores the importance of staying informed about regulatory obligations in the rental property market.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

A landlord in Chester has been fined over £10,000 after being found guilty of managing rental properties without the necessary licence. Carl Southall, 60, was convicted at Mold Magistrates’ Court for 15 offences related to unlicensed property management activities across multiple locations. This case serves as a reminder of the importance of adhering to legal requirements in the rental market.

Details of the Offences

The offences took place in Shotton, Connah’s Quay, and Queensferry, dating back to March 2025. Southall was found to have collected rent from tenants at properties on Wellington Street in Shotton, High Street in Connah’s Quay, and Chester Road West in Queensferry, despite not holding the required licence. Initially, Southall pleaded guilty but later withdrew his pleas, leading the case to proceed to trial. Magistrates imposed fines of £450 for each of the 15 offences, amounting to a total of £6,750.

Financial Penalties and Repayment

In addition to the fines, Southall was ordered to pay £1,750 in prosecution costs and a victim surcharge of £2,000. This brings the overall amount payable to £10,500. The court has mandated that this sum be repaid at a rate of £1,750 per month. This case highlights the financial consequences that can arise from failing to comply with property management regulations.

Landlords and property managers may wish to ensure they hold the appropriate licences and adhere to all legal requirements to avoid similar penalties. This case underscores the importance of staying informed about regulatory obligations in the rental property market.

item.title: Rightmove set to be demoted from FTSE 100
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rightmove, the UK's largest property portal, is poised to exit the FTSE 100 index later this month following a significant drop in its share price over the past year. The anticipated demotion to the FTSE 250, expected to take effect on 22nd June, marks the end of the company's long-standing position among Britain's blue-chip stocks. Housebuilder Berkeley Group Holdings may also face removal from the premier index in the upcoming quarterly reshuffle.

A challenging year for Rightmove

The potential index relegation comes after a turbulent period for Rightmove, with its share price declining by more than 40% in the last twelve months. Several factors have contributed to this downturn, including investor concerns about the company's £60m artificial intelligence investment programme and activist shareholder pressure. The situation was further complicated by a substantial £1.5bn legal claim filed against the business earlier this year.

Rightmove's journey on the London Stock Exchange began in 2006 with a market valuation of approximately £425m. The company grew to become one of the UK's most valuable property businesses, reaching a peak market capitalisation of £6.7bn in 2021 during the pandemic-fuelled housing market boom.

Takeover approaches and market pressures

Last September, Rightmove rejected four acquisition bids from Australian property giant REA Group, with the highest offer valuing the company at £6.2bn. Following REA's decision to withdraw its interest, Rightmove's share price experienced a sharp decline. The stock currently trades well below REA's final offer price, with the company's market value now standing at around £3.3bn.

Market analysts suggest that Rightmove's current valuation reflects broader concerns about the UK property market's outlook, alongside company-specific challenges. The portal's heavy reliance on estate agent subscriptions makes it particularly sensitive to housing market fluctuations and changes in industry sentiment.

Whilst index changes primarily affect institutional investors, homeowners and property professionals might watch these developments as an indicator of market confidence in the UK's property sector. The coming months could prove crucial for Rightmove as it seeks to stabilise its position and demonstrate the value of its strategic investments.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rightmove, the UK's largest property portal, is poised to exit the FTSE 100 index later this month following a significant drop in its share price over the past year. The anticipated demotion to the FTSE 250, expected to take effect on 22nd June, marks the end of the company's long-standing position among Britain's blue-chip stocks. Housebuilder Berkeley Group Holdings may also face removal from the premier index in the upcoming quarterly reshuffle.

A challenging year for Rightmove

The potential index relegation comes after a turbulent period for Rightmove, with its share price declining by more than 40% in the last twelve months. Several factors have contributed to this downturn, including investor concerns about the company's £60m artificial intelligence investment programme and activist shareholder pressure. The situation was further complicated by a substantial £1.5bn legal claim filed against the business earlier this year.

Rightmove's journey on the London Stock Exchange began in 2006 with a market valuation of approximately £425m. The company grew to become one of the UK's most valuable property businesses, reaching a peak market capitalisation of £6.7bn in 2021 during the pandemic-fuelled housing market boom.

Takeover approaches and market pressures

Last September, Rightmove rejected four acquisition bids from Australian property giant REA Group, with the highest offer valuing the company at £6.2bn. Following REA's decision to withdraw its interest, Rightmove's share price experienced a sharp decline. The stock currently trades well below REA's final offer price, with the company's market value now standing at around £3.3bn.

Market analysts suggest that Rightmove's current valuation reflects broader concerns about the UK property market's outlook, alongside company-specific challenges. The portal's heavy reliance on estate agent subscriptions makes it particularly sensitive to housing market fluctuations and changes in industry sentiment.

Whilst index changes primarily affect institutional investors, homeowners and property professionals might watch these developments as an indicator of market confidence in the UK's property sector. The coming months could prove crucial for Rightmove as it seeks to stabilise its position and demonstrate the value of its strategic investments.

item.title: Buyer demand remains weak as housing market struggles for momentum
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market showed signs of stabilisation in May, according to the latest RICS Residential Market Survey, though buyer demand and agreed sales remained weak. The data suggests that whilst the market continues to face challenges, the pace of decline may be slowing. This follows months of subdued activity driven by economic uncertainty and higher borrowing costs.

Buyer Demand and Sales Activity

New buyer enquiries recorded a net balance of -34%, unchanged from April, whilst agreed sales remained in negative territory at -37%. Although these figures indicate a challenging market, the lack of further deterioration suggests activity may be finding a floor. This marks the first month since January where demand did not worsen, offering a glimmer of hope for the months ahead.

However, transaction times continue to lengthen, with the average period from listing to completion now standing at 21.5 weeks. This is the longest delay recorded since RICS began collecting this data in 2017, highlighting ongoing inefficiencies in the market.

House Prices and Regional Trends

House prices remained under pressure, with a net balance of -35% of respondents reporting falls for the second consecutive month. Regional trends varied, with the South East and East Anglia experiencing the weakest price performance. In contrast, Northern Ireland continued to outperform, with surveyors reporting ongoing growth in this region.

Looking ahead, sentiment has improved slightly. Near-term sales expectations rose to -25%, marking an improvement on recent readings. The 12-month outlook moved into positive territory at +2%, suggesting some optimism that market conditions could strengthen over the coming year.

Rental Market Pressures

The rental market continues to face significant challenges, with tenant demand remaining positive at +14% whilst landlord instructions fell further to -28%. This imbalance between supply and demand is driving rental growth, with rent expectations increasing to +36%, their strongest level in a year. This suggests tenants may face further increases in rental costs in the months ahead.

Tarrant Parsons, RICS Head of Market Research and Analysis, commented: “The latest survey data suggest the recent downturn in activity may be beginning to stabilise, with several key indicators broadly holding steady. However, as they remain in negative territory, it would be premature to interpret this as the start of a recovery.”

Whilst the housing market may be showing tentative signs of stabilisation, homeowners and prospective buyers are likely to remain cautious in the face of ongoing economic pressures and the potential for further interest rate rises.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK housing market showed signs of stabilisation in May, according to the latest RICS Residential Market Survey, though buyer demand and agreed sales remained weak. The data suggests that whilst the market continues to face challenges, the pace of decline may be slowing. This follows months of subdued activity driven by economic uncertainty and higher borrowing costs.

Buyer Demand and Sales Activity

New buyer enquiries recorded a net balance of -34%, unchanged from April, whilst agreed sales remained in negative territory at -37%. Although these figures indicate a challenging market, the lack of further deterioration suggests activity may be finding a floor. This marks the first month since January where demand did not worsen, offering a glimmer of hope for the months ahead.

However, transaction times continue to lengthen, with the average period from listing to completion now standing at 21.5 weeks. This is the longest delay recorded since RICS began collecting this data in 2017, highlighting ongoing inefficiencies in the market.

House Prices and Regional Trends

House prices remained under pressure, with a net balance of -35% of respondents reporting falls for the second consecutive month. Regional trends varied, with the South East and East Anglia experiencing the weakest price performance. In contrast, Northern Ireland continued to outperform, with surveyors reporting ongoing growth in this region.

Looking ahead, sentiment has improved slightly. Near-term sales expectations rose to -25%, marking an improvement on recent readings. The 12-month outlook moved into positive territory at +2%, suggesting some optimism that market conditions could strengthen over the coming year.

Rental Market Pressures

The rental market continues to face significant challenges, with tenant demand remaining positive at +14% whilst landlord instructions fell further to -28%. This imbalance between supply and demand is driving rental growth, with rent expectations increasing to +36%, their strongest level in a year. This suggests tenants may face further increases in rental costs in the months ahead.

Tarrant Parsons, RICS Head of Market Research and Analysis, commented: “The latest survey data suggest the recent downturn in activity may be beginning to stabilise, with several key indicators broadly holding steady. However, as they remain in negative territory, it would be premature to interpret this as the start of a recovery.”

Whilst the housing market may be showing tentative signs of stabilisation, homeowners and prospective buyers are likely to remain cautious in the face of ongoing economic pressures and the potential for further interest rate rises.

item.title: Navigating compliance charges under the Renters’ Rights reforms
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Landlords across England are facing fresh financial pressures as letting agents introduce new charges related to Renters' Rights Act compliance. Reports suggest administration fees for updated tenancy paperwork now range between £200-£400, sparking debate about transparency and proportionality in the sector.

Understanding the compliance cost drivers

The Renters' Rights legislation has introduced multiple new requirements for private landlords, including:

  • Updated tenancy documentation templates
  • Revised notice periods and procedures
  • Mandatory government information sheets for tenants
  • New record-keeping obligations

Letting agents argue these changes necessitate substantial operational adjustments. For portfolio landlords or agencies managing hundreds of properties, the administrative burden can be considerable - involving system updates, staff training and document reprocessing across entire tenancy books.

Assessing fee fairness and transparency

Landlords receiving unexpected compliance charges might consider:

  • Reviewing their existing management agreement for any mention of legislative update fees
  • Requesting a detailed breakdown of work undertaken
  • Comparing charges between different agents for similar services
  • Checking whether the fee reflects actual administrative work or appears disproportionate

The Property Ombudsman scheme notes that while agents can charge for genuine compliance work, fees should be reasonable and properly explained. Landlords may wish to question charges that seem excessive compared to the service provided.

As the rental sector adapts to these reforms, both landlords and agents face growing compliance burdens. Open communication about fee structures and a shared understanding of legislative requirements could help navigate this transitional period more smoothly.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Landlords across England are facing fresh financial pressures as letting agents introduce new charges related to Renters' Rights Act compliance. Reports suggest administration fees for updated tenancy paperwork now range between £200-£400, sparking debate about transparency and proportionality in the sector.

Understanding the compliance cost drivers

The Renters' Rights legislation has introduced multiple new requirements for private landlords, including:

  • Updated tenancy documentation templates
  • Revised notice periods and procedures
  • Mandatory government information sheets for tenants
  • New record-keeping obligations

Letting agents argue these changes necessitate substantial operational adjustments. For portfolio landlords or agencies managing hundreds of properties, the administrative burden can be considerable - involving system updates, staff training and document reprocessing across entire tenancy books.

Assessing fee fairness and transparency

Landlords receiving unexpected compliance charges might consider:

  • Reviewing their existing management agreement for any mention of legislative update fees
  • Requesting a detailed breakdown of work undertaken
  • Comparing charges between different agents for similar services
  • Checking whether the fee reflects actual administrative work or appears disproportionate

The Property Ombudsman scheme notes that while agents can charge for genuine compliance work, fees should be reasonable and properly explained. Landlords may wish to question charges that seem excessive compared to the service provided.

As the rental sector adapts to these reforms, both landlords and agents face growing compliance burdens. Open communication about fee structures and a shared understanding of legislative requirements could help navigate this transitional period more smoothly.

item.title: Rental stock shortages continue to drive rent growth
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Rental prices are surging in some of the UK’s most affordable areas, driven by a persistent shortage of available properties despite a slowdown in tenant demand. According to recent data from Zoopla, regions with average monthly rents below £750 are experiencing growth rates of around 5%, more than double the national average of 2.1%. Carlisle, Kilmarnock, and Halifax have seen some of the sharpest rises, with increases of 9.1%, 9.0%, and 6.5% respectively. Even with these hikes, rents in these areas remain below the UK average, highlighting the growing pressure on affordable housing markets.

Rental Stock Shortages Persist

The shortage of rental properties continues to be a significant factor behind rising rents. Zoopla reports that every region in the UK has between 20% and 30% fewer homes available to rent compared to pre-pandemic levels. This scarcity is largely attributed to a lack of new investment in the private rented sector, which has limited options for tenants and kept upward pressure on prices. Despite falling tenant enquiries per property—now at 5.6, the lowest since 2020—the imbalance between supply and demand remains a key driver of rental inflation.

Regional Variations in Rental Trends

While affordable markets are seeing sharp rental increases, the picture varies significantly across the country. Several major cities, including Bournemouth, Nottingham, and Birmingham, have experienced rent reductions over the past year, with declines of 1.7%, 1.5%, and 1.1% respectively. In these areas, affordability constraints and weaker demand have limited landlords’ ability to raise prices. London, however, bucks the trend. The capital has seen a 6% increase in tenant enquiries year-on-year, with average rents now reaching £2,206 per month. Annual rental growth in London has accelerated to 2.2%, making it the only region where demand is on the rise.

Affordability Gains and Future Outlook

While rental affordability has improved nationally, with wages growing at 4% annually compared to rental inflation of 2.1%, these gains may prove short-lived. Zoopla warns that without an increase in rental stock, affordability pressures could return. The property portal predicts rents will rise by 2% to 3% over the remainder of 2026, emphasising the need for more investment in the rental market to address ongoing shortages and stabilise prices.

For tenants navigating this challenging landscape, staying informed about local market trends and exploring a range of rental options could help mitigate the impact of rising costs. Meanwhile, policymakers and investors may need to focus on increasing the supply of rental properties to ease the pressure on tenants and create a more balanced market.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Rental prices are surging in some of the UK’s most affordable areas, driven by a persistent shortage of available properties despite a slowdown in tenant demand. According to recent data from Zoopla, regions with average monthly rents below £750 are experiencing growth rates of around 5%, more than double the national average of 2.1%. Carlisle, Kilmarnock, and Halifax have seen some of the sharpest rises, with increases of 9.1%, 9.0%, and 6.5% respectively. Even with these hikes, rents in these areas remain below the UK average, highlighting the growing pressure on affordable housing markets.

Rental Stock Shortages Persist

The shortage of rental properties continues to be a significant factor behind rising rents. Zoopla reports that every region in the UK has between 20% and 30% fewer homes available to rent compared to pre-pandemic levels. This scarcity is largely attributed to a lack of new investment in the private rented sector, which has limited options for tenants and kept upward pressure on prices. Despite falling tenant enquiries per property—now at 5.6, the lowest since 2020—the imbalance between supply and demand remains a key driver of rental inflation.

Regional Variations in Rental Trends

While affordable markets are seeing sharp rental increases, the picture varies significantly across the country. Several major cities, including Bournemouth, Nottingham, and Birmingham, have experienced rent reductions over the past year, with declines of 1.7%, 1.5%, and 1.1% respectively. In these areas, affordability constraints and weaker demand have limited landlords’ ability to raise prices. London, however, bucks the trend. The capital has seen a 6% increase in tenant enquiries year-on-year, with average rents now reaching £2,206 per month. Annual rental growth in London has accelerated to 2.2%, making it the only region where demand is on the rise.

Affordability Gains and Future Outlook

While rental affordability has improved nationally, with wages growing at 4% annually compared to rental inflation of 2.1%, these gains may prove short-lived. Zoopla warns that without an increase in rental stock, affordability pressures could return. The property portal predicts rents will rise by 2% to 3% over the remainder of 2026, emphasising the need for more investment in the rental market to address ongoing shortages and stabilise prices.

For tenants navigating this challenging landscape, staying informed about local market trends and exploring a range of rental options could help mitigate the impact of rising costs. Meanwhile, policymakers and investors may need to focus on increasing the supply of rental properties to ease the pressure on tenants and create a more balanced market.

item.title: Unlicensed landlord ordered to pay more than £10,000
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

A Chester landlord has been ordered to pay over £10,000 in fines and costs after being convicted of managing rental properties without the necessary licence. The case highlights the importance of complying with local licensing schemes designed to protect tenants and maintain housing standards.

Landlord convicted of unlicensed property management

Carl Southall, 60, appeared at Mold Magistrates’ Court facing 15 charges relating to unlicensed property management activities across Flintshire. The offences involved rental properties in Shotton, Connah’s Quay and Queensferry, dating back to March 2025. Despite initially pleading guilty, Southall later withdrew his pleas, leading to a full trial.

The court heard evidence that Southall had collected rent from tenants at multiple addresses whilst not holding the mandatory licence required for property management. The properties included locations on Wellington Street in Shotton, High Street in Connah’s Quay and Chester Road West in Queensferry.

Substantial financial penalties imposed

Magistrates imposed significant financial penalties for the breaches. Southall received fines of £450 for each of the 15 offences, amounting to £6,750 in total. In addition to the fines, he was ordered to pay £1,750 towards prosecution costs and a £2,000 victim surcharge, bringing the overall sum to £10,500.

The court structured the repayment at £1,750 per month, suggesting the defendant has six months to settle the full amount. This case serves as a stark reminder to landlords about the potential consequences of failing to obtain the correct licences for rental properties.

Prospective landlords and property investors might wish to familiarise themselves with local licensing requirements before entering the rental market. Many councils operate selective licensing schemes that require landlords to meet certain standards and obtain permission before letting properties.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

A Chester landlord has been ordered to pay over £10,000 in fines and costs after being convicted of managing rental properties without the necessary licence. The case highlights the importance of complying with local licensing schemes designed to protect tenants and maintain housing standards.

Landlord convicted of unlicensed property management

Carl Southall, 60, appeared at Mold Magistrates’ Court facing 15 charges relating to unlicensed property management activities across Flintshire. The offences involved rental properties in Shotton, Connah’s Quay and Queensferry, dating back to March 2025. Despite initially pleading guilty, Southall later withdrew his pleas, leading to a full trial.

The court heard evidence that Southall had collected rent from tenants at multiple addresses whilst not holding the mandatory licence required for property management. The properties included locations on Wellington Street in Shotton, High Street in Connah’s Quay and Chester Road West in Queensferry.

Substantial financial penalties imposed

Magistrates imposed significant financial penalties for the breaches. Southall received fines of £450 for each of the 15 offences, amounting to £6,750 in total. In addition to the fines, he was ordered to pay £1,750 towards prosecution costs and a £2,000 victim surcharge, bringing the overall sum to £10,500.

The court structured the repayment at £1,750 per month, suggesting the defendant has six months to settle the full amount. This case serves as a stark reminder to landlords about the potential consequences of failing to obtain the correct licences for rental properties.

Prospective landlords and property investors might wish to familiarise themselves with local licensing requirements before entering the rental market. Many councils operate selective licensing schemes that require landlords to meet certain standards and obtain permission before letting properties.

item.title: Rental income rises £14,000 in last quarter
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landlords across the UK have seen a significant boost to their rental incomes, with average annual earnings reaching a record £89,000 according to recent market data. This represents a substantial £14,000 increase compared to the previous quarter, highlighting the continued strength of the private rental sector.

What's driving the rental income surge?

The sharp rise in rental income appears to stem from several key factors:

  • Sustained high demand from tenants across most UK regions
  • Longer average tenancy durations compared to pre-pandemic levels
  • Limited supply of available rental properties in many areas
  • Rising property values supporting higher achievable rents

Industry analysts suggest this trend may continue in the short-to-medium term, particularly in urban centres where demand continues to outstrip supply. The figures also reflect how professional landlords with multiple properties are benefiting from economies of scale.

Considerations for property investors

For those considering entering the buy-to-let market or expanding their existing portfolio, several factors may be worth noting:

  • Higher rental yields could improve mortgage affordability calculations
  • Location remains crucial - regional variations in rental growth persist
  • Tax implications of increased rental income should be carefully assessed
  • Maintenance costs and regulatory requirements continue to rise

Prospective investors might wish to consult both a mortgage adviser and tax specialist to understand how these market changes could affect their individual circumstances. The current environment may present opportunities, but thorough research remains essential.

While these figures paint a positive picture for landlords, tenants continue to face challenges in many parts of the country. The ongoing imbalance between supply and demand suggests the rental market may remain competitive for the foreseeable future.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Landlords across the UK have seen a significant boost to their rental incomes, with average annual earnings reaching a record £89,000 according to recent market data. This represents a substantial £14,000 increase compared to the previous quarter, highlighting the continued strength of the private rental sector.

What's driving the rental income surge?

The sharp rise in rental income appears to stem from several key factors:

  • Sustained high demand from tenants across most UK regions
  • Longer average tenancy durations compared to pre-pandemic levels
  • Limited supply of available rental properties in many areas
  • Rising property values supporting higher achievable rents

Industry analysts suggest this trend may continue in the short-to-medium term, particularly in urban centres where demand continues to outstrip supply. The figures also reflect how professional landlords with multiple properties are benefiting from economies of scale.

Considerations for property investors

For those considering entering the buy-to-let market or expanding their existing portfolio, several factors may be worth noting:

  • Higher rental yields could improve mortgage affordability calculations
  • Location remains crucial - regional variations in rental growth persist
  • Tax implications of increased rental income should be carefully assessed
  • Maintenance costs and regulatory requirements continue to rise

Prospective investors might wish to consult both a mortgage adviser and tax specialist to understand how these market changes could affect their individual circumstances. The current environment may present opportunities, but thorough research remains essential.

While these figures paint a positive picture for landlords, tenants continue to face challenges in many parts of the country. The ongoing imbalance between supply and demand suggests the rental market may remain competitive for the foreseeable future.

item.title: Student landlords selling up in record numbers
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Record numbers of student landlords appear to be exiting the buy-to-let market, with many citing increased regulatory pressures and rising operational costs as key factors in their decision. This trend could significantly reshape the landscape of houses in multiple occupation (HMO) investments across university towns and cities.

Why student landlords are reconsidering

The student rental sector has traditionally been attractive to property investors due to consistent demand and potentially higher yields compared to standard buy-to-lets. However, several challenges have emerged in recent years that may be prompting landlords to reconsider:

  • Stricter licensing requirements for HMOs, including minimum room sizes and safety standards
  • Increased taxation on rental income and capital gains
  • Rising mortgage costs affecting profitability
  • Growing maintenance expenses and energy efficiency requirements

Potential impacts on the rental market

This exodus of student landlords could have several consequences for both the rental market and property investment landscape:

  • Reduced availability of student accommodation in some areas
  • Potential for rental price increases where supply diminishes
  • Opportunities for remaining landlords or institutional investors
  • Possible shift in local property markets as former HMOs return to residential use

For those considering entering or exiting the student rental market, thorough research into local market conditions and professional financial advice could prove invaluable. The changing landscape presents both challenges and potential opportunities for different types of property investors.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Record numbers of student landlords appear to be exiting the buy-to-let market, with many citing increased regulatory pressures and rising operational costs as key factors in their decision. This trend could significantly reshape the landscape of houses in multiple occupation (HMO) investments across university towns and cities.

Why student landlords are reconsidering

The student rental sector has traditionally been attractive to property investors due to consistent demand and potentially higher yields compared to standard buy-to-lets. However, several challenges have emerged in recent years that may be prompting landlords to reconsider:

  • Stricter licensing requirements for HMOs, including minimum room sizes and safety standards
  • Increased taxation on rental income and capital gains
  • Rising mortgage costs affecting profitability
  • Growing maintenance expenses and energy efficiency requirements

Potential impacts on the rental market

This exodus of student landlords could have several consequences for both the rental market and property investment landscape:

  • Reduced availability of student accommodation in some areas
  • Potential for rental price increases where supply diminishes
  • Opportunities for remaining landlords or institutional investors
  • Possible shift in local property markets as former HMOs return to residential use

For those considering entering or exiting the student rental market, thorough research into local market conditions and professional financial advice could prove invaluable. The changing landscape presents both challenges and potential opportunities for different types of property investors.

item.title: Commercial and transactional perspective for investors after the RRA
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The Renters’ Rights Act (RRA) has introduced significant changes to the UK property market, particularly impacting pricing, due diligence, and investment strategies within the living sector. For landlords and property investors, understanding these shifts is crucial to navigating the evolving landscape effectively.

How the RRA Affects Pricing and Investment Strategies

The RRA has brought about tighter regulations, which may influence the cost of property investments. With increased protections for tenants, landlords might need to allocate more funds towards compliance and property maintenance. This could lead to adjustments in rental pricing to ensure profitability whilst meeting legal obligations.

For investors, it could be worth reviewing portfolios to assess the potential impact of these changes. Properties that were previously lucrative might now require additional investment to align with the new standards. Diversifying investments across different property types or regions may help mitigate risks associated with these regulatory shifts.

Due Diligence Considerations Post-RRA

Thorough due diligence has always been essential in property investment, but the RRA has elevated its importance. Investors may wish to pay closer attention to properties’ compliance with safety standards, energy efficiency requirements, and tenant protection measures. Failing to meet these criteria could result in penalties or difficulties in securing tenants.

Additionally, buyers might consider consulting legal and financial advisors to ensure their investments align with the updated regulations. Staying informed about local authority guidelines and potential future legislative changes could also prove beneficial.

Adapting to the New Market Environment

The RRA underscores the need for landlords and investors to adapt to a more tenant-centric market. Whilst this may initially seem challenging, it also presents opportunities to attract long-term, reliable tenants by offering high-quality, compliant housing.

Embracing technology and innovative property management solutions could streamline compliance processes and enhance tenant satisfaction. For example, digital platforms for maintenance requests or energy efficiency monitoring might help landlords meet regulatory requirements more efficiently.

As the property market continues to evolve under the Renters’ Rights Act, staying proactive and informed will be key to successful investment strategies. By understanding the implications of these changes and taking steps to adapt, landlords and investors can navigate the new landscape with confidence.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The Renters’ Rights Act (RRA) has introduced significant changes to the UK property market, particularly impacting pricing, due diligence, and investment strategies within the living sector. For landlords and property investors, understanding these shifts is crucial to navigating the evolving landscape effectively.

How the RRA Affects Pricing and Investment Strategies

The RRA has brought about tighter regulations, which may influence the cost of property investments. With increased protections for tenants, landlords might need to allocate more funds towards compliance and property maintenance. This could lead to adjustments in rental pricing to ensure profitability whilst meeting legal obligations.

For investors, it could be worth reviewing portfolios to assess the potential impact of these changes. Properties that were previously lucrative might now require additional investment to align with the new standards. Diversifying investments across different property types or regions may help mitigate risks associated with these regulatory shifts.

Due Diligence Considerations Post-RRA

Thorough due diligence has always been essential in property investment, but the RRA has elevated its importance. Investors may wish to pay closer attention to properties’ compliance with safety standards, energy efficiency requirements, and tenant protection measures. Failing to meet these criteria could result in penalties or difficulties in securing tenants.

Additionally, buyers might consider consulting legal and financial advisors to ensure their investments align with the updated regulations. Staying informed about local authority guidelines and potential future legislative changes could also prove beneficial.

Adapting to the New Market Environment

The RRA underscores the need for landlords and investors to adapt to a more tenant-centric market. Whilst this may initially seem challenging, it also presents opportunities to attract long-term, reliable tenants by offering high-quality, compliant housing.

Embracing technology and innovative property management solutions could streamline compliance processes and enhance tenant satisfaction. For example, digital platforms for maintenance requests or energy efficiency monitoring might help landlords meet regulatory requirements more efficiently.

As the property market continues to evolve under the Renters’ Rights Act, staying proactive and informed will be key to successful investment strategies. By understanding the implications of these changes and taking steps to adapt, landlords and investors can navigate the new landscape with confidence.

item.title: New partnership to help international students overcome barriers to renting in UK
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

International students in the UK may find it easier to secure rental accommodation following a new partnership between two fintech firms. flatfair and Flaties have joined forces to address common hurdles such as upfront deposits and UK-based guarantor requirements, which often prove challenging for overseas tenants.

Breaking down rental barriers

The collaboration aims to streamline the rental process for international students who frequently face financial obstacles when searching for accommodation. Many landlords traditionally request substantial deposits and a UK guarantor – conditions that can be particularly difficult for those new to the country without established credit histories or local contacts.

The partnership combines flatfair's deposit alternative solution with Flaties' digital rental platform, potentially offering:

  • Reduced upfront costs through deposit replacement schemes
  • Alternative solutions to traditional guarantor requirements
  • Digital tenancy management tools

How the scheme could benefit students

For international students, the initiative might prove particularly valuable during the initial transition to UK life. The academic year typically sees intense competition for student housing, with overseas applicants sometimes at a disadvantage when competing against domestic tenants who can more easily meet standard rental criteria.

Prospective tenants using the service may find they can:

  • Access properties without paying a full cash deposit upfront
  • Submit applications without needing a UK-based guarantor
  • Complete tenancy agreements digitally from overseas

While the scheme doesn't eliminate all rental challenges, it could help level the playing field for international students during what is often a stressful house-hunting process.

As the private rental sector continues to evolve, such innovations may become increasingly important in supporting the UK's diverse tenant population. International students contribute significantly to both the rental market and local economies, and solutions that facilitate their access to housing could benefit all parties involved.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

International students in the UK may find it easier to secure rental accommodation following a new partnership between two fintech firms. flatfair and Flaties have joined forces to address common hurdles such as upfront deposits and UK-based guarantor requirements, which often prove challenging for overseas tenants.

Breaking down rental barriers

The collaboration aims to streamline the rental process for international students who frequently face financial obstacles when searching for accommodation. Many landlords traditionally request substantial deposits and a UK guarantor – conditions that can be particularly difficult for those new to the country without established credit histories or local contacts.

The partnership combines flatfair's deposit alternative solution with Flaties' digital rental platform, potentially offering:

  • Reduced upfront costs through deposit replacement schemes
  • Alternative solutions to traditional guarantor requirements
  • Digital tenancy management tools

How the scheme could benefit students

For international students, the initiative might prove particularly valuable during the initial transition to UK life. The academic year typically sees intense competition for student housing, with overseas applicants sometimes at a disadvantage when competing against domestic tenants who can more easily meet standard rental criteria.

Prospective tenants using the service may find they can:

  • Access properties without paying a full cash deposit upfront
  • Submit applications without needing a UK-based guarantor
  • Complete tenancy agreements digitally from overseas

While the scheme doesn't eliminate all rental challenges, it could help level the playing field for international students during what is often a stressful house-hunting process.

As the private rental sector continues to evolve, such innovations may become increasingly important in supporting the UK's diverse tenant population. International students contribute significantly to both the rental market and local economies, and solutions that facilitate their access to housing could benefit all parties involved.

item.title: UK Property’s Pulp Fiction Moment
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK residential property market appears to be entering an intriguing phase, with recent indicators pointing towards renewed activity. Robust yields, steadily climbing rental prices and unexpectedly resilient property values suggest the sector may be experiencing a notable upturn.

Signs of a market resurgence

Several key factors are contributing to this potential revival in the housing market:

  • Rental yields remain strong across many regions, particularly in city centres and university towns
  • Average rents continue their upward trajectory, with demand outstripping supply in numerous areas
  • Property prices have demonstrated surprising resilience despite economic headwinds

Considerations for property investors

For those contemplating property investment opportunities, the current climate presents both potential advantages and challenges:

  • The combination of rising rents and stable prices could improve cash flow for landlords
  • Regional variations remain significant, with some areas offering better value than others
  • Mortgage rates, whilst having stabilised recently, remain higher than the historic lows of previous years

Whilst these positive indicators suggest an encouraging outlook for the residential property sector, market conditions can change rapidly. Potential investors might wish to conduct thorough research and consider seeking professional financial advice before making any commitments.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK residential property market appears to be entering an intriguing phase, with recent indicators pointing towards renewed activity. Robust yields, steadily climbing rental prices and unexpectedly resilient property values suggest the sector may be experiencing a notable upturn.

Signs of a market resurgence

Several key factors are contributing to this potential revival in the housing market:

  • Rental yields remain strong across many regions, particularly in city centres and university towns
  • Average rents continue their upward trajectory, with demand outstripping supply in numerous areas
  • Property prices have demonstrated surprising resilience despite economic headwinds

Considerations for property investors

For those contemplating property investment opportunities, the current climate presents both potential advantages and challenges:

  • The combination of rising rents and stable prices could improve cash flow for landlords
  • Regional variations remain significant, with some areas offering better value than others
  • Mortgage rates, whilst having stabilised recently, remain higher than the historic lows of previous years

Whilst these positive indicators suggest an encouraging outlook for the residential property sector, market conditions can change rapidly. Potential investors might wish to conduct thorough research and consider seeking professional financial advice before making any commitments.

item.title: Agency warns punitive second-home taxes won’t free up housing
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Second-home tax hikes unlikely to boost housing supply, experts warn

Sharp increases in council tax premiums for second homeowners across Scotland may fail to address housing shortages whilst potentially becoming an ineffective revenue tool, according to property market analysts. With local authorities implementing wildly varying surcharge rates – ranging from 225% in the Scottish Borders to 500% in Midlothian – concerns are growing that these measures represent political gestures rather than practical housing solutions.

Questionable impact on housing availability

Property specialists highlight that the disproportionate targeting of second homes – which represent a tiny fraction of housing stock in some areas – suggests these policies may have minimal effect on overall availability. In Midlothian, for instance, only 35 properties fall into the second-home category, with just two facing the full 600% combined tax burden when including standard council tax.

David Alexander of DJ Alexander Ltd commented: "When you examine the actual numbers affected, it becomes clear these measures are more about optics than outcomes. The notion that penalising a handful of property owners will meaningfully impact a systemic housing shortage doesn't withstand scrutiny."

Consultation concerns and implementation issues

Critics have raised questions about the genuineness of council consultations on these tax hikes, with Edinburgh's recent process being described as "a box-ticking exercise". The city's consultation document reportedly stated the increase would proceed regardless of feedback, with implementation planned for October.

Alexander noted: "When authorities announce consultations whilst simultaneously confirming the policy will proceed unchanged, it undermines trust in the process. This approach risks alienating property owners whilst failing to deliver tangible housing benefits."

With some homeowners potentially facing annual tax bills approaching £28,000 on higher-value properties, industry observers suggest these measures may simply displace second-home ownership rather than converting properties into primary residences. The long-term effectiveness of such policies in addressing Scotland's housing challenges remains uncertain.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Second-home tax hikes unlikely to boost housing supply, experts warn

Sharp increases in council tax premiums for second homeowners across Scotland may fail to address housing shortages whilst potentially becoming an ineffective revenue tool, according to property market analysts. With local authorities implementing wildly varying surcharge rates – ranging from 225% in the Scottish Borders to 500% in Midlothian – concerns are growing that these measures represent political gestures rather than practical housing solutions.

Questionable impact on housing availability

Property specialists highlight that the disproportionate targeting of second homes – which represent a tiny fraction of housing stock in some areas – suggests these policies may have minimal effect on overall availability. In Midlothian, for instance, only 35 properties fall into the second-home category, with just two facing the full 600% combined tax burden when including standard council tax.

David Alexander of DJ Alexander Ltd commented: "When you examine the actual numbers affected, it becomes clear these measures are more about optics than outcomes. The notion that penalising a handful of property owners will meaningfully impact a systemic housing shortage doesn't withstand scrutiny."

Consultation concerns and implementation issues

Critics have raised questions about the genuineness of council consultations on these tax hikes, with Edinburgh's recent process being described as "a box-ticking exercise". The city's consultation document reportedly stated the increase would proceed regardless of feedback, with implementation planned for October.

Alexander noted: "When authorities announce consultations whilst simultaneously confirming the policy will proceed unchanged, it undermines trust in the process. This approach risks alienating property owners whilst failing to deliver tangible housing benefits."

With some homeowners potentially facing annual tax bills approaching £28,000 on higher-value properties, industry observers suggest these measures may simply displace second-home ownership rather than converting properties into primary residences. The long-term effectiveness of such policies in addressing Scotland's housing challenges remains uncertain.

item.title: BTL landlord earnings surge as tenant demand remains strong
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Buy-to-let landlords have seen a significant boost in rental incomes during the first quarter of 2026, according to fresh research from Pegasus Insight. The latest Landlord Trends survey reveals that average annual rental earnings reached £89,000, marking a £14,000 increase from the previous quarter and a £16,000 rise compared to the same period last year.

Strong Tenant Demand Supports Landlord Earnings

The rebound in landlord incomes follows a temporary dip in late 2025, with current figures showing a 20% year-on-year growth. Analysts suggest this upward trend stems from robust tenant demand, high occupancy rates and longer tenancy durations rather than rental price hikes alone.

Supporting data from Pegasus Insight's Tenant Trends research indicates renters are staying put for extended periods. The average tenant has lived in rented accommodation for approximately eight years, with over five years spent in their current property. Notably, 76% expressed satisfaction with their landlord or letting agent's service.

Stability Benefits Both Landlords and Tenants

The research highlights how longer tenancies create mutually beneficial arrangements. For landlords, reduced tenant turnover means lower void periods and fewer administrative costs. Tenants, meanwhile, enjoy greater housing stability without frequent relocation pressures.

Mark Long, managing director of Pegasus Insight, commented: "These findings demonstrate the enduring strength of the private rental sector's fundamentals. While regulatory changes dominate discussions, the landlord-tenant relationship continues showing remarkable resilience."

The report also notes the average landlord portfolio value has grown to £1.69 million, reflecting sustained property price appreciation across the sector. With two-thirds of tenants intending to remain in their current homes after their tenancy agreements expire, the outlook suggests continued stability for buy-to-let investors.

Whilst acknowledging ongoing challenges around affordability and compliance costs, the research paints an encouraging picture for landlords navigating today's property market. The combination of strong tenant demand, lengthening tenancies and rising portfolio values may offer reassurance to those committed to the private rental sector.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Buy-to-let landlords have seen a significant boost in rental incomes during the first quarter of 2026, according to fresh research from Pegasus Insight. The latest Landlord Trends survey reveals that average annual rental earnings reached £89,000, marking a £14,000 increase from the previous quarter and a £16,000 rise compared to the same period last year.

Strong Tenant Demand Supports Landlord Earnings

The rebound in landlord incomes follows a temporary dip in late 2025, with current figures showing a 20% year-on-year growth. Analysts suggest this upward trend stems from robust tenant demand, high occupancy rates and longer tenancy durations rather than rental price hikes alone.

Supporting data from Pegasus Insight's Tenant Trends research indicates renters are staying put for extended periods. The average tenant has lived in rented accommodation for approximately eight years, with over five years spent in their current property. Notably, 76% expressed satisfaction with their landlord or letting agent's service.

Stability Benefits Both Landlords and Tenants

The research highlights how longer tenancies create mutually beneficial arrangements. For landlords, reduced tenant turnover means lower void periods and fewer administrative costs. Tenants, meanwhile, enjoy greater housing stability without frequent relocation pressures.

Mark Long, managing director of Pegasus Insight, commented: "These findings demonstrate the enduring strength of the private rental sector's fundamentals. While regulatory changes dominate discussions, the landlord-tenant relationship continues showing remarkable resilience."

The report also notes the average landlord portfolio value has grown to £1.69 million, reflecting sustained property price appreciation across the sector. With two-thirds of tenants intending to remain in their current homes after their tenancy agreements expire, the outlook suggests continued stability for buy-to-let investors.

Whilst acknowledging ongoing challenges around affordability and compliance costs, the research paints an encouraging picture for landlords navigating today's property market. The combination of strong tenant demand, lengthening tenancies and rising portfolio values may offer reassurance to those committed to the private rental sector.

item.title: One in five landlords don’t trust agents with client money
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New research reveals that nearly 20% of landlords in England have concerns about letting agents' handling of rental payments and deposits. The study by The Letting Partnership, which surveyed 890 landlords, highlights ongoing trust issues in the rental sector despite most property owners generally trusting their agents.

Landlords seek greater financial transparency

The survey found that 19% of landlords lack confidence in agents' management of client money, including rent collection and deposit holding. This suggests that while the majority of working relationships function smoothly, a significant minority of property investors remain uneasy about financial processes.

Communication gaps appear to be a key factor, with 34% of respondents stating their agents fail to clearly explain compliance procedures or professional standards. The research also uncovered low awareness of voluntary industry checks, as 76% of landlords didn't know some agents undergo independent assessments of their financial and operational practices.

Independent verification could boost confidence

Nearly all surveyed landlords (99%) said they would feel more confident in an agent that could demonstrate independently verified compliance. This strong response indicates that transparency around financial safeguards could become an important differentiator in the lettings market.

Chris Mason of The Letting Partnership noted: "Trust remains fundamental in landlord-agent relationships, especially when handling substantial sums and complex regulations. Our findings show that while most partnerships work well, there's clear demand for better evidence of proper financial controls."

Industry experts suggest that agents who proactively share compliance credentials and undergo external audits may gain a competitive advantage. Simple steps like clearly displaying Client Money Protection certification or explaining deposit protection arrangements could help address landlord concerns.

For landlords, these findings highlight the importance of asking agents about their financial safeguards. Checking whether an agent holds relevant certifications or undergoes independent reviews might provide valuable peace of mind when entrusting them with rental income and deposits.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New research reveals that nearly 20% of landlords in England have concerns about letting agents' handling of rental payments and deposits. The study by The Letting Partnership, which surveyed 890 landlords, highlights ongoing trust issues in the rental sector despite most property owners generally trusting their agents.

Landlords seek greater financial transparency

The survey found that 19% of landlords lack confidence in agents' management of client money, including rent collection and deposit holding. This suggests that while the majority of working relationships function smoothly, a significant minority of property investors remain uneasy about financial processes.

Communication gaps appear to be a key factor, with 34% of respondents stating their agents fail to clearly explain compliance procedures or professional standards. The research also uncovered low awareness of voluntary industry checks, as 76% of landlords didn't know some agents undergo independent assessments of their financial and operational practices.

Independent verification could boost confidence

Nearly all surveyed landlords (99%) said they would feel more confident in an agent that could demonstrate independently verified compliance. This strong response indicates that transparency around financial safeguards could become an important differentiator in the lettings market.

Chris Mason of The Letting Partnership noted: "Trust remains fundamental in landlord-agent relationships, especially when handling substantial sums and complex regulations. Our findings show that while most partnerships work well, there's clear demand for better evidence of proper financial controls."

Industry experts suggest that agents who proactively share compliance credentials and undergo external audits may gain a competitive advantage. Simple steps like clearly displaying Client Money Protection certification or explaining deposit protection arrangements could help address landlord concerns.

For landlords, these findings highlight the importance of asking agents about their financial safeguards. Checking whether an agent holds relevant certifications or undergoes independent reviews might provide valuable peace of mind when entrusting them with rental income and deposits.

item.title: Propertymark backs tougher landlord registration rules
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Propertymark Supports Tougher Landlord Registration Rules in Northern Ireland

Propertymark has voiced its support for proposed changes to Northern Ireland's landlord registration system, stating that strengthened regulations could enhance enforcement against non-compliant landlords whilst maintaining tenant protections. The professional body responded to a consultation by the Department for Communities regarding updates to the 2014 Landlord Registration Scheme Regulations, endorsing measures that would require landlords to submit additional property information during registration.

The proposed reforms would mandate landlords to provide documentation including gas safety certificates, electrical installation condition reports, energy performance certificates, and evidence of working smoke and carbon monoxide alarms. Propertymark emphasised that these requirements could help identify properties failing to meet basic safety standards whilst creating a fairer marketplace for responsible landlords.

Balancing Compliance with Practicality

Whilst supporting the principle of tighter regulation, Propertymark cautioned that new requirements should remain proportionate and manageable for landlords and letting agents. Feedback from the organisation's Northern Ireland members indicated strong backing for mandatory gas safety certification in particular, with calls for the registration system to minimise duplication of existing compliance checks.

Henry Griffith, Policy Officer at Propertymark, commented: "A robust registration scheme can help distinguish between landlords maintaining proper standards and those cutting corners. The proposals could strengthen enforcement whilst giving tenants greater confidence in their rented accommodation."

The organisation also advocated for:

  • Clear guidance on letting agents' responsibilities in managing registration paperwork
  • Direct upload capabilities for agents submitting documents on landlords' behalf
  • Improved data sharing between enforcement bodies to target non-compliant properties

Propertymark's response highlights the delicate balance between raising standards in the private rented sector and avoiding excessive administrative burdens that might discourage landlord participation. The proposed changes to Northern Ireland's registration system could serve as a model for strengthening tenant protections without over-regulating responsible property owners.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

Propertymark Supports Tougher Landlord Registration Rules in Northern Ireland

Propertymark has voiced its support for proposed changes to Northern Ireland's landlord registration system, stating that strengthened regulations could enhance enforcement against non-compliant landlords whilst maintaining tenant protections. The professional body responded to a consultation by the Department for Communities regarding updates to the 2014 Landlord Registration Scheme Regulations, endorsing measures that would require landlords to submit additional property information during registration.

The proposed reforms would mandate landlords to provide documentation including gas safety certificates, electrical installation condition reports, energy performance certificates, and evidence of working smoke and carbon monoxide alarms.
