Propertymark emphasised that these requirements could help identify properties failing to meet basic safety standards whilst creating a fairer marketplace for responsible landlords.

Balancing Compliance with Practicality

Whilst supporting the principle of tighter regulation, Propertymark cautioned that new requirements should remain proportionate and manageable for landlords and letting agents. Feedback from the organisation's Northern Ireland members indicated strong backing for mandatory gas safety certification in particular, with calls for the registration system to minimise duplication of existing compliance checks.

Henry Griffith, Policy Officer at Propertymark, commented: "A robust registration scheme can help distinguish between landlords maintaining proper standards and those cutting corners. The proposals could strengthen enforcement whilst giving tenants greater confidence in their rented accommodation."

The organisation also advocated for:

  • Clear guidance on letting agents' responsibilities in managing registration paperwork
  • Direct upload capabilities for agents submitting documents on landlords' behalf
  • Improved data sharing between enforcement bodies to target non-compliant properties

Propertymark's response highlights the delicate balance between raising standards in the private rented sector and avoiding excessive administrative burdens that might discourage landlord participation. The proposed changes to Northern Ireland's registration system could serve as a model for strengthening tenant protections without over-regulating responsible property owners.

item.title: Rightmove set to be demoted from FTSE 100
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rightmove faces FTSE 100 exit amid share price struggles

Property portal Rightmove appears set for demotion from the FTSE 100 index later this month following a challenging year that has seen its share price decline by over 40%. According to FTSE Russell's latest review, the company is likely to move to the FTSE 250 when the quarterly reshuffle takes effect on 22 June. This potential downgrade marks a significant shift for what has been one of the UK's most prominent listed property businesses since its 2006 flotation.

Market turbulence and investor concerns

Rightmove's position in the blue-chip index has come under pressure from multiple fronts. The company recently announced a £60m investment in artificial intelligence capabilities while simultaneously facing activist shareholder pressure. These developments have coincided with a £1.5bn legal claim filed against the business earlier this year, creating uncertainty for investors.

The property platform's shares reached record highs during the 2021 property market boom, achieving a market capitalisation of approximately £6.7bn. However, the company's rejection of multiple takeover approaches from Australia's REA Group in September 2024 - including a £6.2bn valuation - appears to have contributed to the subsequent share price decline. With the stock now trading well below REA's final offer price, Rightmove's current market valuation stands at around £3.3bn.

Broader implications for property sector

Rightmove's potential demotion comes alongside expectations that housebuilder Berkeley Group may also exit the FTSE 100, reflecting wider challenges in the property sector. Market observers might view these developments as indicators of changing conditions for property-related businesses, particularly those exposed to digital platforms and development markets.

For shareholders and market watchers, the coming weeks will reveal whether Rightmove can stabilise its position or whether this marks the beginning of a new chapter for the property listings giant outside the premier index. The company's future strategy and ability to adapt to changing market conditions may prove crucial in determining its longer-term trajectory.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rightmove faces FTSE 100 exit amid share price struggles

Property portal Rightmove appears set for demotion from the FTSE 100 index later this month following a challenging year that has seen its share price decline by over 40%. According to FTSE Russell's latest review, the company is likely to move to the FTSE 250 when the quarterly reshuffle takes effect on 22 June. This potential downgrade marks a significant shift for what has been one of the UK's most prominent listed property businesses since its 2006 flotation.

Market turbulence and investor concerns

Rightmove's position in the blue-chip index has come under pressure from multiple fronts. The company recently announced a £60m investment in artificial intelligence capabilities while simultaneously facing activist shareholder pressure. These developments have coincided with a £1.5bn legal claim filed against the business earlier this year, creating uncertainty for investors.

The property platform's shares reached record highs during the 2021 property market boom, achieving a market capitalisation of approximately £6.7bn. However, the company's rejection of multiple takeover approaches from Australia's REA Group in September 2024 - including a £6.2bn valuation - appears to have contributed to the subsequent share price decline. With the stock now trading well below REA's final offer price, Rightmove's current market valuation stands at around £3.3bn.

Broader implications for property sector

Rightmove's potential demotion comes alongside expectations that housebuilder Berkeley Group may also exit the FTSE 100, reflecting wider challenges in the property sector. Market observers might view these developments as indicators of changing conditions for property-related businesses, particularly those exposed to digital platforms and development markets.

For shareholders and market watchers, the coming weeks will reveal whether Rightmove can stabilise its position or whether this marks the beginning of a new chapter for the property listings giant outside the premier index. The company's future strategy and ability to adapt to changing market conditions may prove crucial in determining its longer-term trajectory.

item.title: MPs call on Reeves to scrap ‘economy-damaging’ stamp duty
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

MPs are urging Chancellor Rachel Reeves to reconsider stamp duty reforms, warning that the current system may be harming housing affordability and economic growth. A cross-party committee has highlighted concerns that the tax creates barriers for first-time buyers whilst limiting mobility in the property market.

Growing Calls for Stamp Duty Reform

The Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee recently published findings suggesting stamp duty in its current form could be counterproductive. Whilst acknowledging its importance as a Treasury revenue stream, the report argues the tax may inadvertently suppress housing market activity.

Key concerns raised include:

  • Reduced affordability for prospective homeowners
  • Restricted movement within the housing market
  • Potential negative impact on economic growth

The committee has recommended launching a consultation before year-end to explore alternatives, including potential replacements or adjustments to existing thresholds and reliefs.

Balancing Reform with Fiscal Responsibility

With homeownership rates declining over two decades, MPs stress the need for careful consideration of any changes. The report cautions against temporary measures, pointing to the post-pandemic stamp duty holiday which contributed to price inflation before demand tapered off.

Committee members emphasised that reforms should:

  • Form part of wider property tax reviews
  • Consider local housing market variations
  • Maintain long-term stability rather than offer short-term fixes

The debate comes as housing affordability continues to challenge many prospective buyers, particularly those without access to family financial support. Industry experts suggest any changes to stamp duty might need to be balanced against other taxation measures to avoid unintended market distortions.

As discussions continue, homeowners and prospective buyers might watch this space for potential developments in property taxation policy. The government faces the complex task of balancing housing market stimulation with sustainable public finances.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

MPs are urging Chancellor Rachel Reeves to reconsider stamp duty reforms, warning that the current system may be harming housing affordability and economic growth. A cross-party committee has highlighted concerns that the tax creates barriers for first-time buyers whilst limiting mobility in the property market.

Growing Calls for Stamp Duty Reform

The Housing, Communities and Local Government Committee recently published findings suggesting stamp duty in its current form could be counterproductive. Whilst acknowledging its importance as a Treasury revenue stream, the report argues the tax may inadvertently suppress housing market activity.

Key concerns raised include:

  • Reduced affordability for prospective homeowners
  • Restricted movement within the housing market
  • Potential negative impact on economic growth

The committee has recommended launching a consultation before year-end to explore alternatives, including potential replacements or adjustments to existing thresholds and reliefs.

Balancing Reform with Fiscal Responsibility

With homeownership rates declining over two decades, MPs stress the need for careful consideration of any changes. The report cautions against temporary measures, pointing to the post-pandemic stamp duty holiday which contributed to price inflation before demand tapered off.

Committee members emphasised that reforms should:

  • Form part of wider property tax reviews
  • Consider local housing market variations
  • Maintain long-term stability rather than offer short-term fixes

The debate comes as housing affordability continues to challenge many prospective buyers, particularly those without access to family financial support. Industry experts suggest any changes to stamp duty might need to be balanced against other taxation measures to avoid unintended market distortions.

As discussions continue, homeowners and prospective buyers might watch this space for potential developments in property taxation policy. The government faces the complex task of balancing housing market stimulation with sustainable public finances.
